Now I have a complete picture of the codebase. Let me plan the migration:

**Current Pages Router files:**
1. `pages/index.js` → Home page with `getServerSideProps` (SSR)
2. `pages/blog/index.js` → Blog listing with `getStaticProps` + ISR
3. `pages/blog/[id].js` → Blog post with `getStaticPaths` + `getStaticProps` + ISR
4. `pages/api/posts/index.js` → API route (GET all, POST create)
5. `pages/api/posts/[id].js` → API route (GET by id, PUT, DELETE)
6. `pages/_app.js` → Custom App (wraps with AppProvider, layout)
7. `pages/_document.js` → Custom Document (HTML structure, fonts)
8. `pages/_error.js` → Custom Error page
9. `pages/404.js` → Custom 404 page

**Shared files:**
10. `components/AppProvider.js` → React context provider
11. `styles/globals.css` → Global styles

**Migration plan for App Router:**

1. **`app/layout.tsx`** - Root layout (replaces `_app.js` and `_document.js`)
   - HTML structure with lang, Head content from `_document.js`
   - Wrap children with `AppProvider`
   - Layout with header, nav, main, footer from `_app.js`
   - Import globals.css
   
2. **`app/page.tsx`** - Home page (replaces `pages/index.js`)
   - Use an async server component with the data fetching inline
   - Use `useRouter` → use `useRouter` from `next/navigation`
   - Head → use metadata export or `<title>` in the component

3. **`app/blog/page.tsx`** - Blog listing (replaces `pages/blog/index.js`)
   - Use `revalidate` in the fetch
   - Remove `useRouter` client-side navigation, use `<Link>` instead
   - Metadata export for head

4. **`app/blog/[id]/page.tsx`** - Blog post (replaces `pages/blog/[id].js`)
   - Use `generateStaticParams` and `generateMetadata`
   - `useRouter` → `next/navigation`

5. **`app/api/posts/route.ts`** - API route (replaces `pages/api/posts/index.js`)
   - Next.js App Router route handlers use `NextRequest` and `NextResponse`

6. **`app/api/posts/[id]/route.ts`** - API route (replaces `pages/api/posts/[id].js`)
   
7. **`app/not-found.tsx`** - Not found page (replaces `404.js`)

8. **`app/error.tsx`** - Error boundary (replaces `_error.js`)

9. **Convert `.js` to `.tsx`/`.ts`** and add proper types

Let me also check if there are any key differences in Next.js 16 that I need to be aware of. The CLAUDE.md says to check `node_modules/next/dist/docs/`. Let me look at that.

Actually, the user said "If a Pages Router API was used that no longer exists in App Router, replace it with the newer version or the new pattern." Let me think about the specific API changes:

1. `next/head` → In App Router, use metadata API (`generateMetadata` or `metadata` export) for most SEO needs. For per-page `<head>` overrides, you can still use them in client components, but the preferred pattern is metadata exports.

2. `next/router` with `useRouter` → `next/navigation` with `useRouter`
   - `router.push()` → `router.push()` (same name, different import)
   - `router.back()` → `router.back()` (same)
   - `router.isFallback` → No direct equivalent in App Router. With `dynamicParams`, you can handle fallback differently. But in an async server component, we don't have `isFallback`. Actually, `isFallback` was a Pages Router concept. In App Router with `dynamicParams` set, pages not generated at build time are rendered on demand - there's no "loading" fallback state, but you can use `loading.tsx` instead.

3. `getServerSideProps` → Server Components (just `async` function component with direct data fetching)
4. `getStaticProps` → Server Components (just `async` function component with direct data fetching and `revalidate` in fetch options or `export const revalidate`)
5. `getStaticPaths` → `generateStaticParams`
6. `next/document` (`Html`, `Head`, `Main`, `NextScript`) → `app/layout.tsx` replaces all of this
7. `_app.js` → `app/layout.tsx`
8. `_error.js` → `app/error.tsx` (error boundary)
9. `_document.js` → Root layout with proper HTML structure

10. API route: `export default function handler(req, res)` → `export async function GET/POST/PUT/DELETE(req: NextRequest)`

On navigation, layouts preserve state, remain interactive, and do not rerender. 44 45 You can define a layout by default exporting a React component from a [`layout` file](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout). The component should accept a `children` prop which can be a page or another [layout](#nesting-layouts). 46 47 For example, to create a layout that accepts your index page as child, add a `layout` file inside the `app` directory: 48 49 56 57 ```tsx filename="app/layout.tsx" switcher 58 export default function DashboardLayout({ 59 children, 60 }: { 61 children: React.ReactNode 62 }) { 63 return ( 64 65 66 {/* Layout UI */} 67 {/* Place children where you want to render a page or nested layout */} 68

The root layout is **required** and must contain `html` and `body` tags. 90 91 ## Creating a nested route 92 93 A nested route is a route composed of multiple URL segments. 1 --- 2 title: Route Handlers 3 nav_title: Route Handlers 4 description: Learn how to use Route Handlers 5 related: 6 title: API Reference 7 description: Learn more about Route Handlers 8 links: 9 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/route 10 - app/guides/backend-for-frontend 11 --- 12 13 ## Route Handlers 14 15 Route Handlers allow you to create custom request handlers for a given route using the Web [Request](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/Request) and [Response](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/Response) APIs. 16 17 24 25 > **Good to know**: Route Handlers are only available inside the `app` directory. They are the equivalent of [API Routes](/docs/pages/building-your-application/routing/api-routes) inside the `pages` directory meaning you **do not** need to use API Routes and Route Handlers together. 26 27 ### Convention 28 29 Route Handlers are defined in a [`route.js|ts` file](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route) inside the `app` directory: 30 31 ```ts filename="app/api/route.ts" switcher 32 export async function GET(request: Request) {} 33 ``` 34 35 ```js filename="app/api/route.js" switcher 36 export async function GET(request) {} 37 ``` 38 39 Route Handlers can be nested anywhere inside the `app` directory, similar to `page.js` and `layout.js`. But there **cannot** be a `route.js` file at the same route segment level as `page.js`. 40 41 ### Supported HTTP Methods 42 43 The following [HTTP methods](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/HTTP/Methods) are supported: `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `PATCH`, `DELETE`, `HEAD`, and `OPTIONS`. If an unsupported method is called, Next.js will return a `405 Method Not Allowed` response. 44 45 ### Extended `NextRequest` and `NextResponse` APIs 46 47 In addition to supporting the native [Request](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/Request) and [Response](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/Response) APIs, Next.js extends them with [`NextRequest`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/next-request) and [`NextResponse`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/next-response) to provide convenient helpers for advanced use cases. 48 49 ### Caching 50 51 Route Handlers are not cached by default. You can, however, opt into caching for `GET` methods. Other supported HTTP methods are **not** cached. To cache a `GET` method, use a [route config option](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route-segment-config#dynamic) such as `export const dynamic = 'force-static'` in your Route Handler file. 52 53 ```ts filename="app/items/route.ts" switcher 54 export const dynamic = 'force-static' 55 56 export async function GET() { 57 const res = await fetch('https://data.mongodb-api.com/...', { 58 headers: { 59 'Content-Type': 'application/json', 60 'API-Key': process.env.DATA_API_KEY, 61 }, 62 }) 63 const data = await res.json() 64 65 return Response.json({ data }) 66 } 67 ``` 68 69 ```js filename="app/items/route.js" switcher 70 export const dynamic = 'force-static' 71 72 export async function GET() { 73 const res = await fetch('https://data.mongodb-api.com/...', { 74 headers: { 75 'Content-Type': 'application/json', 76 'API-Key': process.env.DATA_API_KEY, 77 }, 78 }) 79 const data = await res.json() 80 81 return Response.json({ data }) 82 } 83 ``` 84 85 > **Good to know**: Other supported HTTP methods are **not** cached, even if they are placed alongside a `GET` method that is cached, in the same file. 86 87 #### With Cache Components 88 89 When [Cache Components](/docs/app/getting-started/cache-components) is enabled, `GET` Route Handlers follow the same model as normal UI routes in your application. They run at request time by default, can be prerendered when they don't access dynamic or runtime data, and you can use `use cache` to include dynamic data in the static response. 90 91 **Static example** - doesn't access dynamic or runtime data, so it will be prerendered at build time: 92 93 ```tsx filename="app/api/project-info/route.ts" 94 export async function GET() { 95 return Response.json({ 96 projectName: 'Next.js', 97 }) 98 } 99 ``` 100 101 **Dynamic example** - accesses non-deterministic operations. During the build, prerendering stops when `Math.random()` is called, deferring to request-time rendering: 102 103 ```tsx filename="app/api/random-number/route.ts" 104 export async function GET() { 105 return Response.json({ 106 randomNumber: Math.random(), 107 }) 108 } 109 ``` 110 111 **Runtime data example** - accesses request-specific data. Prerendering terminates when runtime APIs like `headers()` are called: 112 113 ```tsx filename="app/api/user-agent/route.ts" 114 import { headers } from 'next/headers' 115 116 export async function GET() { 117 const headersList = await headers() 118 const userAgent = headersList.get('user-agent') 119 120 return Response.json({ userAgent }) 121 } 122 ``` 123 124 > **Good to know**: Prerendering stops if the `GET` handler accesses network requests, database queries, async file system operations, request object properties (like `req.url`, `request.headers`, `request.cookies`, `request.body`), runtime APIs like [`cookies()`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cookies), [`headers()`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/headers), [`connection()`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/connection), or non-deterministic operations. 125 126 **Cached example** - accesses dynamic data (database query) but caches it with `use cache`, allowing it to be included in the prerendered response: 127 128 ```tsx filename="app/api/products/route.ts" 129 import { cacheLife } from 'next/cache' 130 131 export async function GET() { 132 const products = await getProducts() 133 return Response.json(products) 134 } 135 136 async function getProducts() { 137 'use cache' 138 cacheLife('hours') 139 140 return await db.query('SELECT * FROM products') 141 } 142 ``` 143 144 > **Good to know**: `use cache` cannot be used directly inside a Route Handler body; extract it to a helper function. Cached responses revalidate according to `cacheLife` when a new request arrives. 145 146 ### Special Route Handlers 147 148 Special Route Handlers like [`sitemap.ts`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/sitemap), [`opengraph-image.tsx`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/opengraph-image), and [`icon.tsx`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/app-icons), and other [metadata files](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata) remain static by default unless they use Dynamic APIs or dynamic config options. 149 150 ### Route Resolution 151 152 You can consider a `route` the lowest level routing primitive. 153 154 - They **do not** participate in layouts or client-side navigations like `page`. 155 - There **cannot** be a `route.js` file at the same route as `page.js`. 156 157 | Page | Route | Result | 158 | -------------------- | ------------------ | ---------------------------- | 159 | `app/page.js` | `app/route.js` | Conflict | 160 | `app/page.js` | `app/api/route.js` | Valid | 161 | `app/[user]/page.js` | `app/api/route.js` | Valid | 162 163 Each `route.js` or `page.js` file takes over all HTTP verbs for that route. 164 165 ```ts filename="app/page.ts" switcher 166 export default function Page() { 167 return

Hello, Next.js!

168 } 169 170 // Conflict 171 // `app/route.ts` 172 export async function POST(request: Request) {} 173 ``` 174 175 ```js filename="app/page.js" switcher 176 export default function Page() { 177 return

Hello, Next.js!

178 } 179 180 // Conflict 181 // `app/route.js` 182 export async function POST(request) {} 183 ``` 184 185 Read more about how Route Handlers [complement your frontend application](/docs/app/guides/backend-for-frontend), or explore the Route Handlers [API Reference](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route). 186 187 ### Route Context Helper 188 189 In TypeScript, you can type the `context` parameter for Route Handlers with the globally available [`RouteContext`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route#route-context-helper) helper: 190 191 ```ts filename="app/users/[id]/route.ts" switcher 192 import type { NextRequest } from 'next/server' 193 194 export async function GET(_req: NextRequest, ctx: RouteContext<'/users/[id]'>) { 195 const { id } = await ctx.params 196 return Response.json({ id }) 197 } 198 ``` 199 200 > **Good to know** 201 > 202 > - Types are generated during `next dev`, `next build` or `next typegen`. 203

For example, to fetch the title and description for a specific blog post: 72 73 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 74 import type { Metadata, ResolvingMetadata } from 'next' 75 76 type Props = { 77 params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 78 searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 79 } 80 81 export async function generateMetadata( 82 { params, searchParams }: Props, 83 parent: ResolvingMetadata 84 ): Promise { 85 const slug = (await params).slug 86 87 // fetch post information 88 const post = await fetch(`https://api.vercel.app/blog/${slug}`).then((res) => 89 res.json() 90 ) 91 92 return { 93 title: post.title, 94 description: post.description, 95 } 96 } 97 98 export default function Page({ params, searchParams }: Props) {} 99 ``` 100 101 ```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 102 export async function generateMetadata({ params, searchParams }, parent) { 103 const slug = (await params).slug 104 105 // fetch post information 106 const post = await fetch(`https://api.vercel.app/blog/${slug}`).then((res) => 107 res.json() 108 ) 109 110 return { 111 title: post.title, 112 description: post.description, 113 } 114 } 115 116 export default function Page({ params, searchParams }) {} 117 ``` 118 119 ### Streaming metadata 120 121 For dynamically rendered pages, Next.js streams metadata separately, injecting it into the HTML once `generateMetadata` resolves, without blocking UI rendering. 122 123 Streaming metadata improves perceived performance by allowing visual content to stream first. 124 125 Streaming metadata is **disabled for bots and crawlers** that expect metadata to be in the `` tag (e.g. `Twitterbot`, `Slackbot`, `Bingbot`). With this mechanism, `fetch` calls using `GET` or `HEAD` with the same URL and options in a single render pass are combined into one request. This happens automatically, and you can [opt out](/docs/app/guides/caching#opting-out) by passing an Abort signal to `fetch`. 241 242 Request memoization is scoped to the lifetime of a request. 243 244 You can also deduplicate `fetch` requests by using Next.js’ [Data Cache](/docs/app/guides/caching#data-cache), for example by setting `cache: 'force-cache'` in your `fetch` options. 245 246 Data Cache allows sharing data across the current render pass and incoming requests. 247 248 If you are _not_ using `fetch`, and instead using an ORM or database directly, you can wrap your data access with the [React `cache`](https://react.dev/reference/react/cache) function. 249 250 ```tsx filename="app/lib/data.ts" switcher 251 import { cache } from 'react' 252 import { db, posts, eq } from '@/lib/db' 253 254 export const getPost = cache(async (id: string) => { 255 const post = await db.query.posts.findFirst({ 256 where: eq(posts.id, parseInt(id)), 257 }) 258 }) 259 ``` 260 261 ```jsx filename="app/lib/data.js" switcher 262 import { cache } from 'react' 263 import { db, posts, eq } from '@/lib/db' 264 import { notFound } from 'next/navigation' 265 266 export const getPost = cache(async (id) => { 267 const post = await db.query.posts.findFirst({ 268 where: eq(posts.id, parseInt(id)), 269 }) 270 }) 271 ``` 272 273 ## Streaming 274 275 > **Warning:** The content below assumes the [`cacheComponents` config option](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheComponents) is enabled in your application. The flag was introduced in Next.js 15 canary. 276 277 When you fetch data in Server Components, the data is fetched and rendered on the server for each request. If you have any slow data requests, the whole route will be blocked from rendering until all the data is fetched. 278 279 To improve the initial load time and user experience, you can use streaming to break up the page's HTML into smaller chunks and progressively send those chunks from the server to the client. 280 281 288 289 There are two ways you can leverage streaming in your application: 290 291 1. Wrapping a page with a [`loading.js` file](#with-loadingjs) 292 2. Wrapping a component with [``](#with-suspense) 293 294 ### With `loading.js` 295 296 You can create a `loading.js` file in the same folder as your page to stream the **entire page** while the data is being fetched. For example, to stream `app/blog/page.js`, add the file inside the `app/blog` folder. 297 298 305 306 ```tsx filename="app/blog/loading.tsx" switcher 307 export default function Loading() { 308 // Define the Loading UI here 309 return
Loading...

The new content will then be automatically swapped in once rendering is complete. 321 322 329 330 Behind-the-scenes, `loading.js` will be nested inside `layout.js`, and will automatically wrap the `page.js` file and any children below in a `` boundary. 331 332 339 340 This approach works well for route segments (layouts and pages), but for more granular streaming, you can use ``. 341 342 ### With `` 343 344 `` allows you to be more granular about what parts of the page to stream. For the best user experience, we recommend designing loading states that are meaningful and help users understand the app is responding. So each segment starts fetching data as soon as possible. 485 486 However, within _any_ component, multiple `async`/`await` requests can still be sequential if placed after the other. By the time `` is rendered, its data has already been fetched. 575 576 ```tsx filename="app/item/[id]/page.tsx" switcher 577 import { getItem, checkIsAvailable } from '@/lib/data' 578 579 export default async function Page({ 580 params, 581 }: { 582 params: Promise<{ id: string }> 583 }) { 584 const { id } = await params 585 // starting loading item data 586 preload(id) 587 // perform another asynchronous task 588 const isAvailable = await checkIsAvailable() 589 590 return isAvailable ? : null 591 } 592 593 const preload = (id: string) => { 594 // void evaluates the given expression and returns undefined 595 // https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Operators/void 596 void getItem(id) 597 } 598 599 export async function Item({ id }: { id: string }) { 600 const result = await getItem(id) 601 // ... 602 } 603 ``` 604 605 ```jsx filename="app/item/[id]/page.js" switcher 606 import { getItem, checkIsAvailable } from '@/lib/data' 607 608 export default async function Page({ params }) { 609 const { id } = await params 610 // starting loading item data 611 preload(id) 612 // perform another asynchronous task 613 const isAvailable = await checkIsAvailable() 614 615 return isAvailable ? : null 616 } 617 618 const preload = (id) => { 619 // void evaluates the given expression and returns undefined 620 // https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Operators/void 621 void getItem(id) 622 } 623 624 export async function Item({ id }) { 625 const result = await getItem(id) 626 // ... 627 ``` 628 629 Additionally, you can use React's [`cache` function](https://react.dev/reference/react/cache) and the [`server-only` package](https://www.npmjs.com/package/server-only) to create a reusable utility function. 1 --- 2 title: Linking and Navigating 3 description: Learn how the built-in navigation optimizations work, including prefetching, prerendering, and client-side navigation, and how to optimize navigation for dynamic routes and slow networks. 4 related: 5 links: 6 - app/api-reference/components/link 7 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/loading 8 - app/guides/prefetching 9 --- 10 11 In Next.js, routes are rendered on the server by default. This often means the client has to wait for a server response before a new route can be shown. Next.js comes with built-in [prefetching](#prefetching), [streaming](#streaming), and [client-side transitions](#client-side-transitions) ensuring navigation stays fast and responsive. 12 13 This guide explains how navigation works in Next.js and how you can optimize it for [dynamic routes](#dynamic-routes-without-loadingtsx) and [slow networks](#slow-networks). 14 15 ## How navigation works 16 17 To understand how navigation works in Next.js, it helps to be familiar with the following concepts: 18 19 - [Server Rendering](#server-rendering) 20 - [Prefetching](#prefetching) 21 - [Streaming](#streaming) 22 - [Client-side transitions](#client-side-transitions) 23 24 ### Server Rendering 25 26 In Next.js, [Layouts and Pages](/docs/app/getting-started/layouts-and-pages) are [React Server Components](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/server-components) by default. On initial and subsequent navigations, the [Server Component Payload](/docs/app/getting-started/server-and-client-components#how-do-server-and-client-components-work-in-nextjs) is generated on the server before being sent to the client. 27 28 There are two types of server rendering, based on _when_ it happens: 29 30 - **Static Rendering (or Prerendering)** happens at build time or during [revalidation](/docs/app/getting-started/caching-and-revalidating) and the result is cached. 31 - **Dynamic Rendering** happens at request time in response to a client request. 32 33 The trade-off of server rendering is that the client must wait for the server to respond before the new route can be shown. Next.js addresses this delay by [prefetching](#prefetching) routes the user is likely to visit and performing [client-side transitions](#client-side-transitions). 34 35 > **Good to know**: HTML is also generated for the initial visit. 36 37 ### Prefetching 38 39 Prefetching is the process of loading a route in the background before the user navigates to it. This makes navigation between routes in your application feel instant, because by the time a user clicks on a link, the data to render the next route is already available client side. 40 41 Next.js automatically prefetches routes linked with the [`` component](/docs/app/api-reference/components/link) when they enter the user's viewport. 42 43 ```tsx filename="app/layout.tsx" switcher 44 import Link from 'next/link' 45 46 export default function Layout({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) { 47 return ( 48 49 50
51 {/* Prefetched when the link is hovered or enters the viewport */} 52 Blog 53 {/* No prefetching */} 54 Contact 55
56 {children} 57 58 59 ) 60 } 61 ``` 62 63 ```jsx filename="app/layout.js" switcher 64 import Link from 'next/link' 65 66 export default function Layout() { 67 return ( 68 69 70
71 {/* Prefetched when the link is hovered or enters the viewport */} 72 Blog 73 {/* No prefetching */} 74 Contact 75
76 {children} 77 78 79 ) 80 } 81 ``` 82 83 How much of the route is prefetched depends on whether it's static or dynamic: 84 85 - **Static Route**: the full route is prefetched. 86 - **Dynamic Route**: prefetching is skipped, or the route is partially prefetched if [`loading.tsx`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/loading) is present. 87 88 By skipping or partially prefetching dynamic routes, Next.js avoids unnecessary work on the server for routes the users may never visit. However, waiting for a server response before navigation can give the users the impression that the app is not responding. 89 90 97 98 To improve the navigation experience to dynamic routes, you can use [streaming](#streaming). 99 100 ### Streaming 101 102 Streaming allows the server to send parts of a dynamic route to the client as soon as they're ready, rather than waiting for the entire route to be rendered. This means users see something sooner, even if parts of the page are still loading. 103 104 For dynamic routes, it means they can be **partially prefetched**. That is, shared layouts and loading skeletons can be requested ahead of time. 105 106 113 114 To use streaming, create a `loading.tsx` in your route folder: 115 116 123 124 ```tsx filename="app/dashboard/loading.tsx" switcher 125 export default function Loading() { 126 // Add fallback UI that will be shown while the route is loading. 127 return 128 } 129 ``` 130 131 ```jsx filename="app/dashboard/loading.js" switcher 132 export default function Loading() { 133 // Add fallback UI that will be shown while the route is loading. 134 return 135 } 136 ``` 137 138 Behind the scenes, Next.js will automatically wrap the `page.tsx` contents in a `` boundary. The prefetched fallback UI will be shown while the route is loading, and swapped for the actual content once ready. 139 140 > **Good to know**: You can also use [``](https://react.dev/reference/react/Suspense) to create loading UI for nested components. 141 142 Benefits of `loading.tsx`: 143 144 - Immediate navigation and visual feedback for the user. 145 - Shared layouts remain interactive and navigation is interruptible. 146 - Improved Core Web Vitals: [TTFB](https://web.dev/articles/ttfb), [FCP](https://web.dev/articles/fcp), and [TTI](https://web.dev/articles/tti). 147 148 To further improve the navigation experience, Next.js performs a [client-side transition](#client-side-transitions) with the `` component. 149 150 ### Client-side transitions 151 152 Traditionally, navigation to a server-rendered page triggers a full page load. This clears state, resets scroll position, and blocks interactivity. 153 154 Next.js avoids this with client-side transitions using the `` component. Instead of reloading the page, it updates the content dynamically by: 155 156 - Keeping any shared layouts and UI. 157 - Replacing the current page with the prefetched loading state or a new page if available. 158 159 Client-side transitions are what makes a server-rendered apps _feel_ like client-rendered apps. And when paired with [prefetching](#prefetching) and [streaming](#streaming), it enables fast transitions, even for dynamic routes. 160 161 ## What can make transitions slow? 162 163 These Next.js optimizations make navigation fast and responsive. However, under certain conditions, transitions can still _feel_ slow. Here are some common causes and how to improve the user experience: 164 165 ### Dynamic routes without `loading.tsx` 166 167 When navigating to a dynamic route, the client must wait for the server response before showing the result. This can give the users the impression that the app is not responding. 168 169 We recommend adding `loading.tsx` to dynamic routes to enable partial prefetching, trigger immediate navigation, and display a loading UI while the route renders. 170 171 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/loading.tsx" switcher 172 export default function Loading() { 173 return 174 } 175 ``` 176 177 ```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/loading.js" switcher 178 export default function Loading() { 179 return 180 } 181 ``` 182 183 > **Good to know**: In development mode, you can use the Next.js Devtools to identify if the route is static or dynamic. See [`devIndicators`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/devIndicators) for more information. 184 185 ### Dynamic segments without `generateStaticParams` 186 187 If a [dynamic segment](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes) could be prerendered but isn't because it's missing [`generateStaticParams`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-static-params), the route will fallback to dynamic rendering at request time. 188 189 Ensure the route is statically generated at build time by adding `generateStaticParams`: 190 191 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 192 export async function generateStaticParams() { 193 const posts = await fetch('https://.../posts').then((res) => res.json()) 194 195 return posts.map((post) => ({ 196 slug: post.slug, 197 })) 198 } 199 200 export default async function Page({ 201 params, 202 }: { 203 params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 204 }) { 205 const { slug } = await params 206 // ... 207 } 208 ``` 209 210 ```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 211 export async function generateStaticParams() { 212 const posts = await fetch('https://.../posts').then((res) => res.json()) 213 214 return posts.map((post) => ({ 215 slug: post.slug, 216 })) 217 218 export default async function Page({ params }) { 219 const { slug } = await params 220 // ... 221 } 222 ``` 223 224 ### Slow networks 225 226 On slow or unstable networks, prefetching may not finish before the user clicks a link. This can affect both static and dynamic routes. In these cases, the `loading.js` fallback may not appear immediately because it hasn't been prefetched yet. 227 228 To improve perceived performance, you can use the [`useLinkStatus` hook](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/use-link-status) to show immediate feedback while the transition is in progress. 229 230 ```tsx filename="app/ui/loading-indicator.tsx" switcher 231 'use client' 232 233 import { useLinkStatus } from 'next/link' 234 235 export default function LoadingIndicator() { 236 const { pending } = useLinkStatus() 237 return ( 238 239 ) 240 } 241 ``` 242 243 ```jsx filename="app/ui/loading-indicator.js" switcher 244 'use client' 245 246 import { useLinkStatus } from 'next/link' 247 248 export default function LoadingIndicator() { 249 const { pending } = useLinkStatus() 250 return ( 251 252 ) 253 } 254 ``` 255 256 You can "debounce" the hint by adding an initial animation delay (e.g. 100ms) and starting as invisible (e.g. `opacity: 0`). This means the loading indicator will only be shown if the navigation takes longer than the specified delay. See the [`useLinkStatus` reference](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/use-link-status#gracefully-handling-fast-navigation) for a CSS example. 257 258 > **Good to know**: You can use other visual feedback patterns like a progress bar. View an example [here](https://github.com/vercel/react-transition-progress). 259 260 ### Disabling prefetching 261 262 You can opt out of prefetching by setting the `prefetch` prop to `false` on the `` component. This is useful to avoid unnecessary usage of resources when rendering large lists of links (e.g. an infinite scroll table). 263 264 ```tsx 265 266 Blog 267 268 ``` 269 270 However, disabling prefetching comes with trade-offs: 271 272 - **Static routes** will only be fetched when the user clicks the link. 273 - **Dynamic routes** will need to be rendered on the server first before the client can navigate to it. 274 275 To reduce resource usage without fully disabling prefetch, you can prefetch only on hover. This limits prefetching to routes the user is more _likely_ to visit, rather than all links in the viewport. 276 277 ```tsx filename="app/ui/hover-prefetch-link.tsx" switcher 278 'use client' 279 280 import Link from 'next/link' 281 import { useState } from 'react' 282 283 function HoverPrefetchLink({ 284 href, 285 children, 286 }: { 287 href: string 288 children: React.ReactNode 289 }) { 290 const [active, setActive] = useState(false) 291 292 return ( 293 setActive(true)} 297 > 298 {children} 299 300 ) 301 } 302 ``` 303 304 ```jsx filename="app/ui/hover-prefetch-link.js" switcher 305 'use client' 306 307 import Link from 'next/link' 308 import { useState } from 'react' 309 310 function HoverPrefetchLink({ href, children }) { 311 const [active, setActive] = useState(false) 312 313 return ( 314 setActive(true)} 318 > 319 {children} 320 321 ) 322 } 323 ``` 324 325 ### Hydration not completed 326 327 `` is a Client Component and must be hydrated before it can prefetch routes. On the initial visit, large JavaScript bundles can delay hydration, preventing prefetching from starting right away. 328 329 React mitigates this with Selective Hydration and you can further improve this by: 330 331 - Using the [`@next/bundle-analyzer`](/docs/app/guides/package-bundling#nextbundle-analyzer-for-webpack) plugin to identify and reduce bundle size by removing large dependencies. 332 - Moving logic from the client to the server where possible. See the [Server and Client Components](/docs/app/getting-started/server-and-client-components) docs for guidance. 333 334 ## Examples 335 336 ### Native History API 337 338 Next.js allows you to use the native [`window.history.pushState`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/History/pushState) and [`window.history.replaceState`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/History/replaceState) methods to update the browser's history stack without reloading the page. 339 340 `pushState` and `replaceState` calls integrate into the Next.js Router, allowing you to sync with [`usePathname`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/use-pathname) and [`useSearchParams`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/use-search-params). 341 342 #### `window.history.pushState` 343 344 Use it to add a new entry to the browser's history stack. The user can navigate back to the previous state. For example, to sort a list of products: 345 346 ```tsx fileName="app/ui/sort-products.tsx" switcher 347 'use client' 348 349 import { useSearchParams } from 'next/navigation' 350 351 export default function SortProducts() { 352 const searchParams = useSearchParams() 353 354 function updateSorting(sortOrder: string) { 355 const params = new URLSearchParams(searchParams.toString()) 356 params.set('sort', sortOrder) 357 window.history.pushState(null, '', `?${params.toString()}`) 358 } 359 360 return ( 361 <> 362 363 364 365 ) 366 } 367 ``` 368 369 ```jsx fileName="app/ui/sort-products.js" switcher 370 'use client' 371 372 import { useSearchParams } from 'next/navigation' 373 374 export default function SortProducts() { 375 const searchParams = useSearchParams() 376 377 function updateSorting(sortOrder) { 378 const params = new URLSearchParams(searchParams.toString()) 379 params.set('sort', sortOrder) 380 window.history.pushState(null, '', `?${params.toString()}`) 381 } 382 383 return ( 384 <> 385 386 387 388 ) 389 } 390 ``` 391 392 #### `window.history.replaceState` 393 394 Use it to replace the current entry on the browser's history stack. The user is not able to navigate back to the previous state. 1 --- 2 title: Server and Client Components 3 description: Learn how you can use React Server and Client Components to render parts of your application on the server or the client. 4 related: 5 title: Next Steps 6 description: Learn more about the APIs mentioned in this page. 7 links: 8 - app/api-reference/directives/use-client 9 --- 10 11 By default, layouts and pages are [Server Components](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/server-components), which lets you fetch data and render parts of your UI on the server, optionally cache the result, and stream it to the client. When you need interactivity or browser APIs, you can use [Client Components](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/use-client) to layer in functionality. 12 13 This page explains how Server and Client Components work in Next.js and when to use them, with examples of how to compose them together in your application. 14 15 ## When to use Server and Client Components? 16 17 The client and server environments have different capabilities. Server and Client components allow you to run logic in each environment depending on your use case. 18 19 Use **Client Components** when you need: 20 21 - [State](https://react.dev/learn/managing-state) and [event handlers](https://react.dev/learn/responding-to-events). E.g. `onClick`, `onChange`. 22 - [Lifecycle logic](https://react.dev/learn/lifecycle-of-reactive-effects). E.g. `useEffect`. 23 - Browser-only APIs. E.g. `localStorage`, `window`, `Navigator.geolocation`, etc. 24 - [Custom hooks](https://react.dev/learn/reusing-logic-with-custom-hooks). 25 26 Use **Server Components** when you need: 27 28 - Fetch data from databases or APIs close to the source. 29 - Use API keys, tokens, and other secrets without exposing them to the client. 30 - Reduce the amount of JavaScript sent to the browser. 31 - Improve the [First Contentful Paint (FCP)](https://web.dev/fcp/), and stream content progressively to the client. 32 33 For example, the `` component is a Server Component that fetches data about a post, and passes it as props to the `` which handles client-side interactivity. 34 35 ```tsx filename="app/[id]/page.tsx" highlight={1,17} switcher 36 import LikeButton from '@/app/ui/like-button' 37 import { getPost } from '@/lib/data' 38 39 export default async function Page({ 40 params, 41 }: { 42 params: Promise<{ id: string }> 43 }) { 44 const { id } = await params 45 const post = await getPost(id) 46 47 return ( 48

The rendering work is split into chunks, by individual route segments ([layouts and pages](/docs/app/getting-started/layouts-and-pages)): 103 104 - **Server Components** are rendered into a special data format called the React Server Component Payload (RSC Payload). 105 - **Client Components** and the RSC Payload are used to [pre-render](/docs/app/guides/caching#rendering-strategies) HTML. 106 107 > **What is the React Server Component Payload (RSC)?** 108 > 109 > The RSC Payload is a compact binary representation of the rendered React Server Components tree. It's used by React on the client to update the browser's DOM. The RSC Payload contains: 110 > 111 > - The rendered result of Server Components 112 > - Placeholders for where Client Components should be rendered and references to their JavaScript files 113 > - Any props passed from a Server Component to a Client Component 114 115 ### On the client (first load) 116 117 Then, on the client: 118 119 1. **HTML** is used to immediately show a fast non-interactive preview of the route to the user. 120 2. **RSC Payload** is used to reconcile the Client and Server Component trees. 121 3. **JavaScript** is used to hydrate Client Components and make the application interactive. 122 123 > **What is hydration?** 124 > 125 > Hydration is React's process for attaching [event handlers](https://react.dev/learn/responding-to-events) to the DOM, to make the static HTML interactive. 126 127 ### Subsequent Navigations 128 129 On subsequent navigations: 130 131 - The **RSC Payload** is prefetched and cached for instant navigation. 132 - **Client Components** are rendered entirely on the client, without the server-rendered HTML. 133 134 ## Examples 135 136 ### Using Client Components 137 138 You can create a Client Component by adding the [`"use client"`](https://react.dev/reference/react/use-client) directive at the top of the file, above your imports. 139 140 ```tsx filename="app/ui/counter.tsx" highlight={1} switcher 141 'use client' 142 143 import { useState } from 'react' 144 145 export default function Counter() { 146 const [count, setCount] = useState(0) 147 148 return ( 149
150

{count} likes

153 ) 154 } 155 ``` 156 157 ```jsx filename="app/ui/counter.js" highlight={1} switcher 158 'use client' 159 160 import { useState } from 'react' 161 162 export default function Counter() { 163 const [count, setCount] = useState(0) 164 165 return ( 166
167

{count} likes

168 169
170 ) 171 } 172 ``` 173 174 `"use client"` is used to declare a **boundary** between the Server and Client module graphs (trees). 175 176 Once a file is marked with `"use client"`, **all its imports and child components are considered part of the client bundle**. This means you don't need to add the directive to every component that is intended for the client. 177 178 ### Reducing JS bundle size 179 180 To reduce the size of your client JavaScript bundles, add `'use client'` to specific interactive components instead of marking large parts of your UI as Client Components. 181 182 For example, the `` component contains mostly static elements like a logo and navigation links, but includes an interactive search bar. `` is interactive and needs to be a Client Component, however, the rest of the layout can remain a Server Component. 183 184 ```tsx filename="app/layout.tsx" highlight={12} switcher 185 // Client Component 186 import Search from './search' 187 // Server Component 188 import Logo from './logo' 189 190 // Layout is a Server Component by default 191 export default function Layout({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) { 192 return ( 193 <> 194

This means it's possible to accidentally import server-only code into the client. For example, consider the following function: 705 706 ```ts filename="lib/data.ts" switcher 707 export async function getData() { 708 const res = await fetch('https://external-service.com/data', { 709 headers: { 710 authorization: process.env.API_KEY, 711 }, 712 }) 713 714 return res.json() 715 } 716 ``` 717 718 ```js filename="lib/data.js" switcher 719 export async function getData() { 720 const res = await fetch('https://external-service.com/data', { 721 headers: { 722 authorization: process.env.API_KEY, 723 }, 724 }) 725 726 return res.json() 727 } 728 ``` 729 730 This function contains an `API_KEY` that should never be exposed to the client. 731 732 In Next.js, only environment variables prefixed with `NEXT_PUBLIC_` are included in the client bundle. However, if your linting rules flag extraneous dependencies, you may install them to avoid issues. 759 760 ```bash package="npm" 761 npm install server-only 762 ``` 763 764 ```bash package="yarn" 765 yarn add server-only 766 ``` 767 768 ```bash package="pnpm" 769 pnpm add server-only 770 ``` 771 772 ```bash package="bun" 773 bun add server-only 774 ``` 775 776 Next.js handles `server-only` and `client-only` imports internally to provide clearer error messages when a module is used in the wrong environment. The contents of these packages from NPM are not used by Next.js. 777 778 Next.js also provides its own type declarations for `server-only` and `client-only`, for TypeScript configurations where [`noUncheckedSideEffectImports`](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/#noUncheckedSideEffectImports) is active. 779

All tasks complete. Here's a detailed summary of the migration:

## Migration Summary

### Files Created (all TypeScript)

| File | Replaces | Key Changes |
|---|---|---|
| `app/layout.tsx` | `_app.js` + `_document.js` | Combined App Provider wrapper with HTML structure; `React.ReactNode` children type |
| `app/page.tsx` | `pages/index.js` | Async server component using `headers()` instead of `getServerSideProps`; `metadata` export for `<head>`; `<Link>` instead of `router.push` |
| `app/blog/page.tsx` | `pages/blog/index.js` | Async server component with `fetch({ next: { revalidate: 60 } })` instead of `getStaticProps`; `<Link>` replaces programmatic navigation |
| `app/blog/[id]/page.tsx` | `pages/blog/[id].js` | `generateStaticParams` replaces `getStaticPaths`; `generateMetadata` replaces `<Head>`; `notFound()` replaces `notFound: true` return; `params: Promise<{id: string}>` |
| `app/api/posts/route.ts` | `pages/api/posts/index.js` | Named `GET`/`POST` exports replace `export default handler`; `NextRequest`/`NextResponse` replace `req`/`res` |
| `app/api/posts/[id]/route.ts` | `pages/api/posts/[id].js` | Named `GET`/`PUT`/`DELETE` exports; `params: Promise<>` pattern |
| `app/not-found.tsx` | `pages/404.js` | Server component; `<Link>` replaces `router.push('/')` |
| `app/error.tsx` | `_error.js` | Client component (`'use client'`); `error`/`reset` boundary pattern replaces `getInitialProps` |
| `app/blog/loading.tsx` | *(new)* | Loading UI for dynamic blog navigation |
| `app/globals.css` | `styles/globals.css` | Moved into `app/` per convention |
| `components/AppProvider.tsx` | `AppProvider.js` | Added `'use client'` directive, `ReactNode`, and full TypeScript interface types |

### Files Deleted
- `pages/` (entire directory — `index.js`, `blog/index.js`, `blog/[id].js`, `_app.js`, `_document.js`, `_error.js`, `404.js`, `api/posts/index.js`, `api/posts/[id].js`)
- `styles/globals.css` (moved to `app/globals.css`)
- `components/AppProvider.js` (replaced by `.tsx`)

### API Migration Patterns
- **`next/head`** → `metadata` / `generateMetadata` exports (SEO data, not per-page `<Head>`)
- **`next/router` `useRouter`** → `next/link` `<Link>` for navigation; `next/navigation` for programmatic cases
- **`useRouter().isFallback`** → handled by `loading.tsx` + `Suspense` boundaries
- **`getServerSideProps`** → async server component + `headers()`/`cookies()` from `next/headers`
- **`getStaticProps`** → async server component + `fetch` options for revalidation
- **`getStaticPaths`** → `generateStaticParams`
- **`next/document` (`Html`, `Head`, `Main`, `NextScript`)** → root `layout.tsx` with `<html>`/`<body>` tags
- **API `req`/`res` (Pages Router)** → `NextRequest`/`NextResponse` with named HTTP method exports
